subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s Degree in Political Science from a U.S. university
Completed coursework in research methods, statistics, and data analysis
Gained exposure to neuroscience topics through electives and lab internships
Participated in undergraduate research assisting with data collection and analysis
Attended short workshops on Python programming during college

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Works as a Researcher in a neuroscience laboratory
Focuses on pediatric brain cancer diagnosis and treatment research
Role emphasizes data analysis and development of computational tools
Collaborates closely with a supervisor and occasionally with a larger research team

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Employed in the biomedical research sector
Works at the intersection of neuroscience and healthcare
Lab is affiliated with a major hospital
Contributes to translational research aiming to improve clinical outcomes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works in a lab of approximately 12 people
Day-to-day work environment is a small, focused team (self and supervisor)
Lab is part of a larger hospital research ecosystem
Collaborative setting with access to hospital-collected data and occasional interdepartmental meetings

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Non-profit academic medical research laboratory
Specializes in pediatric brain cancer research
Serves hospitals, academic partners, and the broader scientific community
Focuses on translational research that bridges laboratory findings and patient care

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as a researcher
Started current full-time role shortly after completing undergraduate studies
Gained practical lab experience through internships before current position

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Entry-level research professional
Reports directly to a supervisor or PI (Principal Investigator)
Not in a managerial or supervisory role
Occasionally provides technical insight to more senior researchers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Designs and codes software tools for data analysis in Python
Analyzes brain imaging and clinical data provided by hospital partners
Collaborates with supervisor to discuss project requirements and research goals
Documents code, analysis results, and research progress
Participates in meetings to provide computational insights to lab colleagues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 70% of work hours coding and performing data analysis
Around 15% of time is devoted to meetings and team collaboration
Roughly 10% is spent on documentation and reporting
The remaining 5% involves administrative tasks such as time card entry

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has autonomy to select coding approaches and toolkits for analysis
Responsible for planning, prototyping, and implementing software solutions
Major research directions and priorities are set by supervisor or PI
Can independently resolve technical challenges within project boundaries

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Routine data cleaning and preprocessing in Python
Implementing basic analysis scripts for standard datasets
Writing documentation for code developed
Entering and updating time cards based on calendar records

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Developing novel algorithms for extracting features from medical images
Debugging intricate code errors in large or unfamiliar codebases
Translating ambiguous project requirements into concrete technical specifications
Ensuring data integrity and reproducibility in complex analyses

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Reformatting and reprocessing large batches of imaging data
Generating standard plots and summary statistics for recurring analyses
Updating metadata and maintaining data logs
Creating template code for repeated analysis pipelines

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys designing new analysis tools and solving complex data problems
Finds collaborative problem-solving in meetings intellectually stimulating
Considers repetitive data entry and time card submission tedious
Routine code maintenance feels less engaging than developing new features

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual data formatting is time-consuming and error-prone
Occasional integration issues between hospital data systems and lab analysis tools
Limited documentation for legacy code can slow down debugging
Waiting for feedback or approvals on analysis plans can cause bottlenecks

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels most productive when working on tasks aligned with personal strengths, such as coding new analysis modules
Enjoyment is highest when learning or applying new skills to challenging problems
Productivity drops during repetitive or administrative tasks
Skill level and confidence increase with autonomy and opportunities for creative problem-solving

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Has a positive attitude toward experimenting with new software and coding libraries
Views learning new technologies as essential for both personal and professional growth
Actively seeks out new tools that improve workflow efficiency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new programming environments and data analysis platforms
Finds it manageable to pick up new Python libraries or AI tools with minimal guidance
Occasionally requires extra time to adapt to major changes in workflow or unfamiliar tool interfaces

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate several hours per month to learning new tools or methods
Prioritizes tech training that directly relates to current research projects
Sometimes postpones training when project deadlines are tight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential to automate repetitive tasks and improve research outcomes
Barriers include high workload during grant deadlines or major project milestones
Prefers learning tools with clear documentation and community support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Supervisor’s endorsement of new tools increases willingness to adopt
Peers occasionally recommend new libraries or AI tools
Team culture values technical innovation, positively influencing openness to change

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Python 3 as the primary programming language for data analysis
Codes and edits scripts in VS Code Editor
Occasionally uses Matlab for specific analysis tasks
Employs Jupyter notebooks for exploratory data analysis and visualization

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Relies on hospital data management systems for accessing patient data
Uses Microsoft Excel for preliminary data review and organization
Employs Git for version control and code collaboration
Utilizes institutional email and calendar applications for communication and scheduling

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Comfortable with widely used Python libraries such as NumPy, pandas, and matplotlib
Has basic familiarity with Matlab and its toolboxes for neuroscience research
Understands use of Jupyter notebooks for combining code, results, and documentation
Basic experience with version control systems such as Git and GitHub

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally encounters data format incompatibilities between hospital systems and analysis scripts
Manual conversion is sometimes needed to move data between Excel and Python
Limited integration between Matlab and Python workflows requires exporting and importing files
Version discrepancies between software tools can lead to minor workflow disruptions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts such as supervised learning and model training
Can explain terms such as 'neural network', 'feature extraction', and 'classification'
Has practical experience using AI-powered code assistants
Limited exposure to advanced machine learning theory beyond practical applications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses GitHub Copilot for code autocompletion and suggestions
Employs ChatGPT for generating or debugging simple code snippets
Has experimented with other generative AI chatbots for brainstorming or technical support
No direct use of custom in-house AI models for research (currently focused on mainstream tools)

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses AI tools weekly in the workflow
Primarily employs generative AI to generate boilerplate or repetitive code
Occasionally uses AI to help troubleshoot coding errors or identify alternative algorithms
Rarely leverages AI for full end-to-end pipeline automation—focus is on support rather than replacement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI tools have successfully generated functional code for repetitive tasks, speeding up workflow
Occasionally received syntactically correct but contextually inappropriate code suggestions from AI
Learned to double-check AI-generated code for compatibility and accuracy before use
AI has helped clarify unfamiliar library usage by providing concise examples

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal organizational training provided for AI tool adoption
Peers occasionally share tips for using Copilot and ChatGPT effectively
Relies on online resources and documentation to learn AI tool features
Supervisor is supportive but not an expert in AI-assisted coding

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers to work independently, consulting AI tools as needed for specific tasks
Will occasionally use step-by-step interactions with AI for complex coding problems
Values having the option to request granular explanations from AI when troubleshooting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI tools primarily as assistants for routine or repetitive coding
Occasionally treats AI as an advisor for brainstorming alternative approaches
Does not rely on AI as a full co-worker or autonomous collaborator

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to integrating new AI-driven processes if they clearly improve efficiency
Prefers incremental adoption over wholesale workflow changes
Willing to test AI-powered tools in pilot projects before broader use

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Finds conversational interfaces (like ChatGPT) intuitive for brainstorming and troubleshooting
Uses command-based code assistants (like Copilot) for in-line code suggestions
Prefers conversational AI when seeking explanations or learning new concepts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many analysis tasks require neuroscience and medical domain expertise
AI tools are mainly used for generic programming, not for specialized research insights
Critical decisions about data interpretation depend on human expertise
AI is not trusted for final result validation in clinical research contexts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Moderate trust in AI-generated code for routine, well-defined problems
Low trust in AI output for high-stakes or novel research tasks
Always manually reviews and tests AI-generated code before integration
Reluctant to delegate critical methodological decisions to AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle repetitive coding and data formatting tasks
Values human oversight for experimental design and data interpretation
Sees greatest potential in AI augmenting, not replacing, expert judgment
Envisions a workflow where AI suggestions are always subject to human approval

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for low-impact, repetitive processes
Finds automation threatening if applied to tasks requiring nuanced domain judgment
Would resist automation that bypasses manual review for clinical or patient-related data
Supports automation that improves speed but not at the expense of accuracy or transparency

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the importance of critical thinking and problem-solving skills
Routine coding and data entry skills may become less critical as automation increases
Domain expertise remains essential for interpreting AI-generated results
AI frees up time to focus on higher-level research questions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing skills in prompt engineering when interacting with generative AI
Learning to evaluate and debug AI-generated code effectively
Staying current with evolving AI tool capabilities and best practices
Gaining responsibility for integrating AI tools into existing research pipelines

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mild concern about automation of repetitive coding tasks
No immediate worry about AI replacing research roles requiring domain expertise
Monitors industry trends about AI in research to stay informed
Views AI as a tool for augmentation rather than replacement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always manually reviews AI-generated code before execution
Maintains the ability to edit or reject AI suggestions in code editors
No AI-driven process is deployed without human validation in current workflow
Favors systems where override and rollback options are easily accessible

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Team has become more open to piloting AI tools over the past year
No formal, organization-wide AI policy in place yet
Peer discussions increasingly focus on responsible AI use in research
Supervisor encourages careful experimentation with new AI technologies

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will drive major advances in biomedical research and healthcare
Expects increased efficiency and productivity across scientific fields
Concerned about potential for bias and misapplication of AI in medicine
Sees AI as a catalyst for more interdisciplinary collaboration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports ethical guidelines for AI use in clinical research
Worries about transparency and explainability of AI-driven decisions
Believes that human oversight is critical for safeguarding patient data
Advocates for clear documentation of AI tool limitations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires AI tools that better integrate with hospital data systems
Would benefit from explainable AI that clarifies decision logic for analysis recommendations
Seeks AI assistants that automate documentation and reporting with contextual awareness
Wishes for AI-driven quality assurance to flag potential errors in analysis pipelines

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine coding and data cleaning to become mostly automated
Anticipates spending more time on experimental design and interpretation of results
Foresees greater collaboration with data scientists and AI specialists
Believes maintaining technical adaptability will be essential for career growth

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Implement structured training programs for AI tool adoption
Develop clear policies for ethical AI use in research and patient data handling
Pilot new AI solutions with small teams before broader rollout
Foster open discussions among staff about AI’s benefits and limitations
